items.text: Did you have any warning, like nausea, sweating, or tunnel vision, before you passed out, or did it happen with no warning at all?
choices: Warning prodrome with nausea, diaphoresis, or tunnel vision
No prodrome, sudden loss of consciousness

items.text: Were you standing for a long time, in the heat, in pain, or scared right before it happened, or was it triggered by coughing, urinating, or having a bowel movement?
choices: Prolonged standing, heat, pain, or fear

items.text: Were you lying down, sitting, standing up, or exercising when it happened?
How long were you out, and did you feel back to normal right away or stay confused afterward?
choices: Brief, under one minute, with rapid full recovery
Prolonged postictal confusion after regaining consciousness

items.text: Did anyone see you jerk or shake, bite your tongue, or lose control of your bladder during the episode?
Did you feel your heart racing or pounding right before you passed out?
Did you have chest pain right before you passed out?
Have you been told you have a heart valve problem, a thickened heart muscle, or a weak heart?
choices: Known valvular disease or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
Known reduced ejection fraction or implanted defibrillator

items.text: Has anyone in your family died suddenly from a heart problem before age 50?
choices: Family history of sudden cardiac death before age 50

items.text: Have you started a new blood pressure pill, water pill, or heart rhythm medication recently?
Have you noticed black, tarry stools, red blood in your stool, or vomited blood?
Did you hit your head when you fell?
Do you have diabetes, and did you feel shaky, sweaty, or hungry right before this happened?
choices: Known diabetes with hypoglycemic symptoms preceding the event

items.text: Have you been immobilized, had recent surgery or travel, or do you have cancer or a history of blood clots?
choices: Active malignancy or prior venous thromboembolism

items.text: Assess orthostatic vital signs when safe.
Auscultate the heart for a murmur.
Assess heart rate and rhythm.
Auscultate for a carotid bruit.
Assess for a focal neurologic deficit.
Inspect the tongue for a lateral laceration suggesting seizure.
Inspect for injury sustained during the fall.
Perform a rectal examination when gastrointestinal bleeding is suspected.
choices: Normal or deferred as not indicated

items.text: Inspect conjunctiva and nail beds for pallor.
Assess skin for diaphoresis or pallor at the time of evaluation.
